steps.title: Welcome to OpenCatalogi
steps.body: A couple of quick steps to get you publishing. OpenCatalogi turns your catalogs into open, discoverable publications — let's create your first catalog and connect you to the federated network.

steps.title: Publishing prerequisites
steps.body: OpenCatalogi publishes from OpenRegister. The catalog, publication and listing registers are configured automatically on install — this just verifies them. If OpenRegister was enabled after OpenCatalogi, running this re-imports the configuration.

steps.title: Default catalog scope
steps.body: Publishing in OpenCatalogi is governed by schema-RBAC (not a per-object published flag), and every catalog needs a scope. Pick the default visibility for the catalog you're about to create — you can still tighten or widen it later.

steps.title: Create your first catalog
steps.body: A catalog is the container your publications live in. We'll create your first one using the scope you just chose — you can rename it and add publications afterwards.

steps.title: Connect to the federated network
steps.body: OpenCatalogi is a federated network: instances share their published catalogs so they can be discovered together. Connect to the national directory now to see federated catalogs in your Directory — or skip this and it will sync automatically later.

steps.title: All set
steps.body: OpenCatalogi is ready. Open Catalogs to manage what you publish, or the Directory to discover federated catalogs.

steps.title: Welcome to OpenCatalogi
steps.body: Let's walk the publishing journey together — from an empty install to a catalog the world can discover. You'll create each record yourself, in your own workspace.
Everything you publish lives in a catalog. Open Catalogs from the menu.
Create your first catalog — it's the container your publications will live in.
Open the catalog you just created to manage what it publishes — its publications open inside it.
steps.task: Open your catalog and view its Publications

steps.body: A publication is a single thing you make open — a document, dataset or record. Add your first one.

steps.title: Publishing is access-controlled
steps.body: OpenCatalogi has no separate 'publish' switch — visibility is governed by schema-RBAC and the catalog's scope. Grant the public (or a group) read access on the publication schema, within the catalog scope you chose, and the publication becomes discoverable.
Published catalogs are found through the Directory — your own and federated ones. Open it to see the discovery side.

steps.title: You're publishing
steps.body: That's the full loop: catalog → publication → schema-RBAC publishing → discovery. Externally, your published catalogs are harvested as DCAT-AP, so registers and portals can find and re-list your publications automatically.

observability._note: ADR-040 declarative observability. OpenCatalogi adopts OpenRegister's AppHost engine: the /api/health and /api/metrics routes are aliased to AppHost\Controller\GenericHealth#index / GenericMetrics#index in appinfo/routes.php, and the engine reads this block at request time. The hand-written HealthController + MetricsController were deleted. Parity vs the pre-adoption contract: the two health checks (database critical, filesystem degraded) and search_requests_total map 1:1 to declarative descriptors; the JSON-field-grouped domain metrics (publications/catalogs/listings by status+catalog, directory entries, and the usageCounter view/download sums by catalog) are reproduced byte-for-byte by OpenCatalogiMetricsProvider via the {kind:provider} escape hatch, which runs the exact same OpenRegister-backed queries the deleted controller ran. Documented intentional improvements (matching OpenRegister's own adoption): the health JSON now carries an `app` field; the implicit opencatalogi_up gauge is engine-owned (always 1 once the request is served) rather than mirroring the DB check.

metrics.name: domain
metrics.help: OpenCatalogi domain metrics (publications, catalogs, listings, directory entries, usage view/download totals) reproduced via the provider escape hatch with the same OpenRegister-backed queries as the pre-adoption MetricsController.

pages.title: WOO batches
pages._note: WOO disclosure-batch list via the generic index renderer over the WOO register/schema (ADR-022 — reuses the lib, no bespoke table). Per-batch detail (deck board widget + WOO tabs) is the WooBatchDetail custom page.

pages.title: WOO batch
pages._note: Surfaces the OpenRegister deck-board widget (queue/board — not bespoke) plus WOO-specific surfaces: progress summary, redaction review, inventarislijst download, ready-for-review/publish (approval-workflow gated).

pages.title: Dashboard
pages._note: Custom analytics dashboard (charts + recent activity + per-catalog stats). Could be type:"dashboard" with widget composition once chart widgets land.

pages.title: Catalog
pages._note: CnDetailPage with bespoke header (catalog stats + slug + edit actions) + sidebar tabs. Lib gap: header-component slot resolution from manifest, granular sidebar-tab declaration.

pages.title: Publication
pages._note: DCAT-AP publication record — the app's core artifact and the reason OpenCatalogi exists. Publication/document archetype: someone lands here to (1) read/edit the WOO metadata, (2) manage the distributables, (3) check publish/retention state. ADR-062 rev-3 rework: the primary Data card was SPLIT into two field-scoped Data widgets so neither clips — 'Publication' (identity/publishing: title, summary, description, publicatiedatum, depublicatiedatum, status) and 'Retention' (the RET-003/006 statutory-retention group: retentionCategory, retentionTermMonths, retentionAction, retentionExpiresAt, retentionNote, retentionLastEvaluatedAt) — both 8-wide, 2-column. The `organization` and `themes[]` fields are excluded from the Data cards because the compact Related panel (4-wide) resolves them; now that publication.organization carries $ref:organization and publication.themes[] items carry $ref:theme, the Related panel renders publisher + theme NAMES instead of raw uuids. The former header `summaryAggregates` KPI chips became an in-grid `stats-block` (top-right, 4-wide): Views + Downloads summed from this publication's usageCounter rows (usageCounter.publication is the FK, now $ref:publication). Below sits the full-width Attachments Files leaf: a publication IS its distributables, so Files (documents/downloads) is the dominant secondary surface, followed by Images (photos) and external Links (bookmarks) side-by-side for the rest of the DCAT distribution set. schema-verified absences: there is NO publicationType schema in this register (only catalog/listing/organization/publication/theme/page/menu/glossary/usageCounter exist), so no publication-type Related row; and the publication schema declares NO linkedTypes, so NO email/calendar/talk/contacts leaves — a publication is metadata about a document, not a correspondence thread (HARD RULE 1). geo/maps dropped — no geo field. lifecycleActions drives the schema's real status transition (published → archived, the RET-006 retention transition — the transitions block exists, so this stays a real lifecycle, not a frozen readOnly field). The audit trail (publications are FOI/WOO records where change history matters legally) lives in the sidebar as widgets:[{type:'audit'}], per the audit-only sidebar rule. Metadata is on-demand via the Data widget's Actions menu (showMetadata) rather than a permanent card.

pages.title: Search
pages._note: FederationSearch (custom) wraps CnSearchPage with the federation-aware search store so /search hits /api/federation/publications. The previous type:'search' default fanned out per-schema against the local OpenRegister, bypassing federation entirely.

pages.title: Organization
pages._note: Publisher-organization archetype — the legal entity behind catalogs/publications (name, summary, description, the government identifiers OIN/TOOI/RSIN/PKI, and a single logo `image`). A user lands here to answer 'who is this publisher and what do they publish?'. The body leads with the identity block (8-wide, 2-column so the four registration codes read cleanly, `image` excluded because the Logo leaf renders it). The right column stacks the in-grid `stats-block` (top: Publications + Catalogs counts, formerly header summaryAggregates) over the Images leaf (4-wide) carrying the logo/branding (the schema's `image` is a single logo, so Images is the right surface, not Files). The 'what do they publish' half is answered with two INBOUND object-lists on the row below: the publications whose `organization` FK points at this record, and the catalogs whose `organization` FK points at it — both FKs now carry $ref:organization so their name display + org picker resolve. A `related` (outbound) widget stays intentionally ABSENT — the organization schema has ZERO outbound refs (every field is a scalar), so a related panel would render empty; the meaningful relations are inbound, which object-list expresses (the petstore parent→children pattern). org-catalogs gets viewAllRoute:'Catalogs' (that index takes no route param, so the deep-link resolves) + rowRoute:'CatalogDetail'; org-publications gets NO viewAllRoute/rowRoute — the Publications index/detail routes both require a :catalogSlug this page can't supply, so a broken deep-link is deliberately omitted. Both lists keep create ON (default): an organization OWNS its publications/catalogs, so creating from the list pre-links the org FK. schema-verified absences: NO contacts/comms leaves — the organization schema declares NO linkedTypes and no contactpersoon relation (HARD RULE 1); no status lifecycle field, so no lifecycleActions. The change-history audit trail (federation syncs matter) lives in the sidebar widgets:[{type:'audit'}] per the audit-only sidebar rule. Metadata is on-demand via the Data widget's Actions menu.

content.emptyText: This organization has no publications yet.
This organization has no catalogs yet.

pages.title: Theme
pages._note: Catalog-theme / presentation archetype — a visual grouping used to categorise and style publications (title, summary, description, image, content, link/url, icon). This is a configuration+content record, so the body pairs the editable fields (6-wide, switched to 2-column so all ten fields fit the cell without an internal scrollbar — ADR-062 rule 1/3) with the live theme-preview custom widget (6-wide, app-registered via registerDashboardWidget) so an editor sees the rendered card/banner as they tune it — the reason theme-preview exists. The former header summaryAggregates KPI became an in-grid `stats-block` (top-right of the preview column: Publications-with-this-theme count). Below, a full-width INBOUND object-list surfaces the publications tagged with this theme (publication.themes[] is a facetable array whose items now carry $ref:theme, so the FK filter {themes:@objectId} resolves and rows show publication names); allowCreate is FALSE here — theme is a many-to-many TAG, not an owner, so creating a publication 'in' a theme (and orphaned from any catalog) is not a valid affordance. A `related` (outbound) widget stays ABSENT — the theme schema has no outbound refs, so it would render empty; the meaningful relation (publications → theme) is inbound and object-list expresses it. Deliberately NO comms and NO Files: a theme communicates with nobody, declares no linkedTypes, and its `image`/`content` live in the record itself (HARD RULE 1/2). Audit history is the sidebar widgets:[{type:'audit'}] per the audit-only sidebar rule; Metadata is on-demand via the Data widget's Actions menu. Row navigation is intentionally omitted on the list — PublicationDetail's route needs a :catalogSlug this page can't supply, so rows display without a broken deep-link.

content.emptyText: No publications are tagged with this theme yet.

pages.title: Glossary term
pages._note: Glossary-term / reference archetype — a single defined concept (title, summary, description, externalLink, keywords) used to explain vocabulary across the catalog. A term's value is its definition plus how it connects to other terms, so the body pairs the definition fields (6-wide) with the relationship-graph custom widget (6-wide, app-registered) that visualises related terms via shared keywords — the reason that widget exists. Intentionally the leanest detail page: NO comms, NO Files, and — verified against the register — NO other schema carries a glossary FK, so there are no inbound refs to list and NO `related`/object-list panel (the previous `related` widget was outbound-empty and was REMOVED; keeping it would have shown a permanent empty card). A glossary entry declares no linkedTypes, has no attachments, and communicates with nobody (HARD RULES 1 & 2). Audit history is the sidebar CnAuditTrailTab; Metadata is on-demand via the Data widget's Actions menu.

pages.title: Page
pages._note: CMS-page / structured-content archetype — a portal page (title, slug, visibility flags hideBeforeLogin/hideAfterLogin, access groups) whose body is an ordered list of embedded content blocks (`contents[]` — inline objects, NOT a separate register schema, so an object-list child table cannot be used). Previously this page had NO widgets and fell back to the generic data dump. Now the body pairs the page settings (5-wide, 1-column — slug + visibility read as a config sheet, with `contents` excluded because the tree-view owns it) with the tree-view custom widget (7-wide) that renders the nested content-block structure so an editor can see and reorder the page composition. NO comms/Files/related: a portal page declares no linkedTypes, its content is inline, and it communicates with nobody (HARD RULE 1). Sidebar is the registry-driven CnObjectSidebar (config.sidebar.enabled); Metadata is on-demand via the Data widget's Actions menu rather than a permanent card.

pages.title: Menu
pages._note: Navigation-menu / structure archetype — a portal menu (title, position, visibility flag hideBeforeLogin, access groups) whose SUBSTANCE is its ordered, nestable sub-menu items. The user comes here to see and reorder those sub-menu items, so the body pairs the menu settings (4-wide, 1-column — position/visibility read as a config sheet, with `items` excluded because the tree-view owns it) with a wide tree-view (8-wide) that renders the ordered item hierarchy and lets an editor reorder it, since the tree IS the menu. IMPORTANT design constraint verified against the register: the sub-menu items live in `menu.items[]` as INLINE objects (each with order/name/link/description/icon/groups) — there is NO separate menu-item register schema and menu has NO self-FK, so the intuitively-desired FK-scoped `object-list` of child menu items ('items where parentMenu=@objectId') is NOT expressible. tree-view over the inline `items` field, sorted by each item's `order`, is the faithful representation of the ordered sub-menu items. NO comms/Files/related: a menu declares no linkedTypes, its items are inline, and it communicates with nobody (HARD RULE 1). Audit history is the sidebar CnAuditTrailTab; Metadata is on-demand via the Data widget's Actions menu. WISH-LIST for nc-vue: an ordered-editor widget over an inline object-array (add/remove/reorder items) would be a better fit than the read-lean tree-view here.

pages.title: Directory
pages._note: FederationDirectory (custom) fetches /api/listings and renders CnFederationStatus with mapped nodes + an admin add-directory modal. The bare CnFederationStatus this replaced shipped a permanent empty-state because the manifest had no :nodes binding.
